trueFeatures: It is an English sacred choral work.
It uses a religious text.
It may be unaccompanied or accompanied by organ or orchestra.
MC-0262.falseFeature: It is a simple solo song often accompanied by lute.

trueFeatures: It is a simple song or melody.
A late-Renaissance English example may be a solo song.
It may be accompanied by lute.
MC-0263.falseFeature: It has a recurring fa-la-la refrain.

trueFeatures: It is a light, dance-like English madrigal.
It has a recurring fa-la-la refrain.
It is usually secular, lively and strophic.
MC-0264.falseFeature: It is a German Lutheran hymn tune.

trueFeatures: It is a German Lutheran hymn tune.
It usually has a clear, regular rhythm.
It is often harmonised homophonically in four SATB parts.
MC-0265.falseFeature: It is produced mainly with computers and electronic instruments for dancing.

trueFeatures: It is a broad term for recent and present-day jazz styles.
Improvisation is an important feature.
It may combine complex rhythms, electronics and influences from other styles.
MC-0266.falseFeature: It is a slow, stately Renaissance court dance in duple metre.

trueFeatures: It is made mainly with electronic instruments and computer technology.
It often uses a strong repetitive beat, loops and programmed drums.
Build-ups and drops are common features.
MC-0267.falseFeature: It is a lively Renaissance court dance in triple metre.

trueFeatures: It is a lively Renaissance court dance.
It is in triple metre.
It often follows a slower pavan and may use hemiola near cadences.
MC-0268.falseFeature: It is a secular vocal work for several unaccompanied voices.

trueFeatures: It is a secular Renaissance vocal work.
It is usually written for several unaccompanied voices.
Word painting, polyphony and through-composed writing are common.
MC-0269.falseFeature: It usually sets a sacred Latin text.

trueFeatures: It is a sacred Renaissance choral work.
It is usually polyphonic and sung in Latin.
Imitation, modal harmony and unaccompanied singing are common.
MC-0270.falseFeature: It deliberately draws on the folk identity of a nation.

trueFeatures: It is art music that deliberately draws on a nation’s folk culture or identity.
It may use traditional melodies, dances, scales or rhythms.
It may use national stories, historical subjects or indigenous instruments.
MC-0271.falseFeature: It revives earlier forms and textures while using modern harmony and rhythm.

trueFeatures: It revives the balance and clarity of Classical or earlier music.
It combines older forms and textures with modern harmony and rhythm.
It often uses smaller ensembles and a clear formal design.
MC-0272.falseFeature: It organises music using a predetermined series instead of functional tonality.

trueFeatures: It is a slow, stately Renaissance court dance.
It is usually in duple metre.
It was often paired with a faster galliard.
MC-0273.falseFeature: It is a lively court dance in triple metre.

trueFeatures: Modal harmony is common.
Flowing polyphonic vocal writing and imitation are common.
Lutes, viols, recorders and early brass are characteristic instruments.
MC-0274.falseFeature: It often uses programmed drums, synthesisers, loops and samples.

trueFeatures: It organises music using a predetermined series.
It avoids traditional functional tonality.
A twelve-tone row may be transposed, inverted or played in retrograde.
MC-0275.falseFeature: It is an English sacred choral work used in Protestant worship.

trueFeatures: It is an expressive non-chord note.
It is approached by leap and usually resolves by step.
It takes some of the following main note's value.
MC-0276.falseFeature: It consists of a root, major third and augmented fifth.

trueFeatures: It is a major triad with its fifth raised by a semitone.
It contains a root, major third and augmented fifth.
It consists of two stacked major thirds and sounds unsettled.
MC-0277.falseFeature: It is the minor triad built on the second degree of a major scale.

trueFeatures: It is a minor triad built on scale degree two in a major key.
Its first inversion places the third of the chord in the bass.
It often leads towards chord V as a predominant harmony.
MC-0278.falseFeature: It uses two or more tonal centres at the same time.

trueFeatures: The chords are built on scale degrees one, four, five and six.
In a major key, I, IV and V are major while VI is minor.
They can create perfect, imperfect, plagal and interrupted cadences.
MC-0279.falseFeature: It orders all twelve chromatic pitches before any pitch is repeated.

trueFeatures: It uses more than one key or tonal centre at the same time.
Bitonality specifically uses two keys at once.
The overlapping keys often create complex clashes.
MC-0280.falseFeature: It circles a main note using the notes immediately above and below it.

trueFeatures: A prepared note is held from one chord into the next.
The held note becomes a dissonance in the new chord.
It usually resolves downwards by step.
MC-0281.falseFeature: It circles the main note using both neighbouring notes.

trueFeatures: It is an ordered arrangement of all twelve chromatic pitch classes.
Each pitch is usually used before any is repeated.
It may be transposed, inverted or reversed.
MC-0282.falseFeature: It is approached by leap and resolves by step into a chord note.

trueFeatures: It spans three whole tones.
It is an augmented fourth or diminished fifth.
It creates strong dissonance and is found in dominant-seventh harmony.
MC-0283.falseFeature: It circles the main note using the notes immediately above and below it.

trueFeatures: It is an ornament built around a main note.
It uses the neighbouring note above and the neighbouring note below.
It returns to the main note after circling it.
MC-0284.falseFeature: It regroups two groups of three as three groups of two.

trueFeatures: It regroups two groups of three as three groups of two, or the reverse.
The underlying note lengths do not change.
It creates temporary metric ambiguity and often appears near cadences.
MC-0285.falseFeature: It restates a fugue subject in the next voice, usually at the dominant.

trueFeatures: In a fugue, it restates the subject in the next voice.
It usually begins a fifth above or a fourth below the subject.
It may be real, with exact intervals, or tonal, with adjusted intervals.
MC-0286.falseFeature: It uses two separated groups that alternate or respond to one another.

trueFeatures: Two separated groups of voices or instruments are used.
The groups alternate or answer one another.
The effect creates a spatial musical dialogue.
MC-0287.falseFeature: It links the first and second subjects in sonata form.

trueFeatures: In sonata form, it links the first and second subjects.
It usually builds momentum and changes key.
It is also called a transition.
MC-0288.falseFeature: It is a recurring counter-melody designed to combine with a fugue subject.

trueFeatures: It is a recurring counter-melody in a fugue.
It often begins after a voice has stated the subject or answer.
It is designed to combine contrapuntally with the subject or answer.
MC-0289.falseFeature: It is a slow Renaissance court dance in duple metre.

trueFeatures: It is a contrapuntal work built from a main subject.
Independent voices enter one after another with the subject and answer.
Later entries may alternate with episodes and use inversion or stretto.
MC-0290.falseFeature: It is a connected group of songs forming one complete work.

trueFeatures: It reverses the direction of each melodic interval.
An upward interval becomes the matching downward interval.
It can transform a melody or a tone row.
MC-0291.falseFeature: It presents the notes in reverse order from last to first.

trueFeatures: It is a recurring musical idea linked to a dramatic subject.
It may represent a character, place, object or emotion.
It may change as the story and its meaning develop.
MC-0292.falseFeature: It is a connected group of songs usually performed in a fixed order.

trueFeatures: It presents a melody or tone row in reverse order.
It begins with the original final note.
In serial music, it may be combined with inversion.
MC-0293.falseFeature: It reverses the direction of each melodic interval.

trueFeatures: It is a connected group of songs forming a complete work.
The songs share a narrative, theme, character or emotional journey.
The songs are often performed in a fixed order.
MC-0294.falseFeature: A new fugue subject entry begins before the previous one has finished.

trueFeatures: It is a technique used in a fugue.
A new subject entry begins before the previous entry has finished.
Overlapping subject entries increase density and may build towards a climax.
MC-0295.falseFeature: It is the main theme first heard alone at the start of a fugue.

trueFeatures: It is the main theme of a fugue.
It is first heard alone.
It returns in other voices as subject or answer entries.
MC-0296.falseFeature: It is an ensemble of instruments associated especially with Renaissance music.

trueFeatures: It is an ensemble of instruments associated especially with Renaissance music.
A whole example uses instruments from one family.
A mixed or broken example combines different instrument families.
MC-0297.falseFeature: It is a high adult male voice usually produced using falsetto.

trueFeatures: It is a high adult male voice.
It is usually produced mainly using falsetto.
Its range is similar to an alto or sometimes a mezzo-soprano.
MC-0298.falseFeature: It is a chamber group made up of piano, violin and cello.

trueFeatures: It consists of piano, violin and cello.
It may mean the chamber ensemble or a work written for those instruments.
The three parts may interact as equal partners.
MC-0299.falseFeature: It is a vocal technique between speaking and singing.

trueFeatures: It is a vocal technique between speaking and singing.
The performer follows written pitch contours or starting pitches.
Pitches are released into a speech-like, dramatic delivery.
MC-0300.falseFeature: It is an English sacred choral work using a religious text.
